edges.description: Basal-ganglia-predominant neuronal GM1 storage drives the extrapyramidal dystonia phenotype.
edges.source: Partial Beta-Galactosidase Deficiency with Basal Ganglia GM1 Accumulation

edges.description: Basal ganglia dysfunction can manifest as parkinsonian extrapyramidal features.
edges.source: Partial Beta-Galactosidase Deficiency with Basal Ganglia GM1 Accumulation

edges.description: Extrapyramidal involvement contributes to speech motor dysfunction.
edges.source: Partial Beta-Galactosidase Deficiency with Basal Ganglia GM1 Accumulation

edges.description: Progressive extrapyramidal dysfunction produces gait disturbance.
edges.source: Partial Beta-Galactosidase Deficiency with Basal Ganglia GM1 Accumulation

edges.target: Partial Beta-Galactosidase Deficiency with Basal Ganglia GM1 Accumulation

nodes.description: Dystonia is the predominant feature of adult GM1 gangliosidosis, reflecting selective basal ganglia involvement.

evidence.snippet: progressive extrapyramidal symptoms, including dystonia and gait disturbance

meta.association: Biallelic GLB1 variants with relatively high residual beta-galactosidase activity

evidence.snippet: GM1 gangliosidosis is a progressive, neurosomatic, lysosomal storage disorder caused by mutations in the GLB1 gene encoding the enzyme β-galactosidase.

nodes.description: Progressive gait disturbance accompanies the extrapyramidal syndrome.
Parkinsonian features can accompany the extrapyramidal syndrome.

evidence.snippet: the Type 3 variant (chronic or adult) is characterized by later symptom onset (between 3 and 30 years of age) and progressive extrapyramidal symptoms

nodes.description: Biallelic GLB1 variants leave relatively high residual lysosomal beta-galactosidase activity. Slow, regionally selective GM1 ganglioside accumulation predominantly in the basal ganglia produces an adult-onset extrapyramidal syndrome with comparatively little somatic storage.
nodes.id: Partial Beta-Galactosidase Deficiency with Basal Ganglia GM1 Accumulation

evidence.snippet: Subsequent accumulation of GM1 ganglioside and other substrates in the lysosome impairs cell physiology and precipitates dysfunction of the nervous system.
